creator: freebsd@byshenk.net
text:
On Mon, Jan 26, 2009 at 04:56:00PM +0000, Robert Watson wrote:
> On Mon, 26 Jan 2009, pluknet wrote:
> 
> >>I can't reproduce what you're seeing on 8.x with the patch, but perhaps 
> >>there's a difference between 7.x and 8.x that I haven't taken into 
> >>account.
> >
> >Eh.. it seems atime/ctime are only modified. Sorry for noise.
> 
> Well, this does raise an interesting question: should we also trim the 
> setting of IN_ACCESS from ffs_extread() to prevent the access time from 
> being updated on extended attribute read?  It sounds a bit like the answer 
> is yes.
 

Ok, for me it does not seem to solve the problem.

7.1-STABLE sources as of 20 Jan 2009, patched as directed, complete
build of the system, but setting an ACL still changes file mtime.


   dev-data-1# ls -l
   total 477530
   -rw-r--r--  1 root  root      21397 Apr 11  2008 ait-tapes
   -rw-r--r--  1 root  root      18949 Feb 29  2008 ait-tapes.20080229
   dev-data-1# find ./ -mtime -1
   ./
   dev-data-1# setfacl -m u:www:rwx ait-tapes
   dev-data-1# ls -l
   total 477532
   -rw-rwxr--+ 1 root  root      21397 Jan 26 19:23 ait-tapes
   -rw-r--r--  1 root  root      18949 Feb 29  2008 ait-tapes.20080229
   dev-data-1# find ./ -mtime -1
   ./
   ./ait-tapes
   dev-data-1# 


It appears that I can _change_ ACLs without updating mtime, which is
good, but applying them changes the mtime.

For example:

   dev-data-1# cp -pR acltest acltestb
   dev-data-1# ls -l acltest*
   acltest:
   total 44
   -rw-rwxr--+ 1 root  root  21397 Jan 26 19:23 ait-tapes
   -rw-r--r--  1 root  root  18949 Feb 29  2008 ait-tapes.20080229
   
   acltestb:
   total 44
   -rw-rwxr--+ 1 root  root  21397 Jan 26 19:31 ait-tapes
   -rw-r--r--  1 root  root  18949 Feb 29  2008 ait-tapes.20080229
   dev-data-1# 

Note that the file without ACLs has its mtime preserved, while the 
file with ACLs has its mtime altered (to "now").

Which means that one cannot actually preserve times when copying
files with ACLs, which is sometimes very irritating.


Or perhaps I'm doing something wrong (though I don't think so).


-- 
greg byshenk  -  gbyshenk@byshenk.net  -  Leiden, NL
